title: Stashing
content:
### What is Stashing?

Stashing temporarily shelves changes so you can work on something else, then reapply them later.

### Basic Stash Operations

```bash
git stash                          # Stash tracked changes
git stash pop                      # Apply + drop from stash
git stash apply                    # Apply but keep in stash
git stash drop                     # Remove a stash entry
git stash list                     # View all stashes
```

### Stashing Untracked Files

By default, `git stash` only stashes tracked files. Include untracked files with:

```bash
git stash -u                       # Include untracked files
git stash --include-untracked      # Long form
git stash -a                       # Include all (including ignored)
```

### Named Stashes

Give stashes a descriptive message:

```bash
git stash push -m "WIP: login form validation"
```

Stashes appear in the list as `stash@{0}: On main: WIP: login form validation`.

### Applying a Specific Stash

```bash
git stash list                     # Shows: stash@{0}, stash@{1}, ...
git stash apply stash@{1}          # Apply a specific stash
git stash drop stash@{1}           # Remove a specific stash
git stash pop stash@{1}            # Apply + remove specific stash
```

### Use Cases

- Switching branches mid-task
- Pulling urgent fixes without committing WIP
- Experimenting with different approaches
codeExample:
echo "work in progress" > feature.txt
git add feature.txt
git stash push -m "WIP: feature"
# Do urgent work, then:
git stash pop
# feature.txt is back

quiz.question: Which flag includes untracked files when stashing?
What does git stash pop do compared to git stash apply?
options: Pop applies to a different branch
